These are the steps, in order, of the snap suggestion formula that we
just covered. Here are the elements we just covered in the last 2

We’ve got the optional personal introduction, which is super-easy. I
don’t think there will be any questions about that, Josh. The pattern
interrupt, that’s where most of the questions come from. We covered
the big promise, in this one. We covered video scarcity, making sure

different. This is not anything you’ve heard before. And then, let’s
make a deal, the transition into step 2.
Let’s open that up.
Josh: 	The first question is from Richard. I’ll paraphrase here. Before, you
said you don’t want to make any big assumptions about what they
think. You said, “Maybe you think you’ve seen this before,” or
whatever. And then he says, “Later on, you used the word ‘never.’
‘You’ve never seen this before, I promise.’” He’s wondering how come
those are different?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

2
Jon: 	That’s a really good question. Because I’m not telling them what they
think. I’m telling them a fact. Honestly, it’s a fact that you’re kind of
pushing. Because if your video sales letter has got some unique stuff
in it, chances are very great they’ve never heard this before.
This is where it gets a little tricky. If you say, “You may have never
heard this before,” you’ll lose them.
Where I want to use the word “may” is when I start talking about the
way they’re thinking, versus what they know, things like that, things
that are more intangible.
So, this is kind of a tangible thing. The only thing I know, right now,
about Richard, because I have nothing in front of me, is that I’m talking
to a guy named Richard. But I can pretty much assume that you’re not
a girl.
So, if I say, “Look, I know that you’re not a girl, I’m not going way out
on a limb there, right? I’m not really superceding my boundaries of
saying that. That’s not going to offend you.
But if I say, “Richard, I know exactly what you’re thinking,” when you
don’t know me, and it’s happening right off the bat, that’s disruptive.
But if I tell you, “Richard, in part 3 of this video tutorial where we’re
going on with video sales letters, I guarantee you you’re going to learn
something you’ve never heard before. And I know that for a fact
because I wrote it. So, unless you’ve been following stuff that I’ve
written and have said in front of an audience, then I know, for a fact,
you haven’t heard it before.” That’s far less intrusive.
That’s a really good question, Richard.
Where you want to use the word “may” is when you’re talking about
how they’re thinking or feeling. Feeling is even more important.
Feeling is crucial. When I say, “Richard, I know you’re feeling a little
confused and disappointed over your weight loss right now. And
Richard, you could be 150 pounds, soaking wet. So, I’m just making
this up. “I know that you’re feeling confused,” and I don’t know that. I
don’t know that. “But I do know that you’ve never heard anything like
I’m about to tell you before.” The chances are at least very great.
That’s the difference.
So, I would say an example for feeling. Feeling is, I would say, even
more important than thinking, especially if your audience is female.
Say, “Look, Richard, you may be feeling a little bit perplexed. Maybe
you’re not.” If I just say, “Maybe you’re not, maybe you’re feeling,”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

3
that’s the secret here. Because chances are you’re going to use
phrases that are what the majority of your users are probably feeling.
I don’t know what industry you’re in, but let’s say that you’re in the legal
industry and you’re helping people get money back on something.
Maybe it’s a personal injury lawyer. You can pretty much assume
someone has been through an injury or a work-related injury, is feeling
frustrated, they’re in pain. Obviously, they have an injury.
So, by you saying, “Look, you may be feeling really frustrated right
now, and in pain over your injury, but…” That’s all you need to say.
That’s a really good question. Glad you brought it up. Thanks,
Richard.

But your USP, I remember it, and Josh just reread it, has something to
do with pulling anything out of the water without cracking the hull of
your boat. Right?
Josh: 	Close enough. Cracking the fiberglass in the boat.
Jon: 	Okay, cracking the fiberglass in your boat. What I would want to know
is what does crack it? So, “I’m going to give you the top 3 things that
crack your fiberglass in your boat, that you probably think is not going
to do it.” You could even go off the topic and say, “I’m going to give
you a great tip on repairing your fiberglass.” That’s kind of a value-
added tip.
I can think of quite a few tips that would work for that. Again, if you
know your audience is wakeboarders, you can say, “I’m even going to
toss in a cool wakeboarding tip for you, because I’m a professional
skier. So, I’ll give you my favorite wakeboard tip.” They’re going to
listen.
So, when it’s a service, or if it’s a product that meets a need on a mass
market, like this, you can relate right to the desire. Remember, this is
all built around the desires of your viewers. The desire of your viewer,
at the point they hit your video sales letter, is certainly, “Hey, I want to
protect my boat. Hey, I’m tired of something cracking my fiberglass.”
But, what’s beyond that desire, Lane? They want to ski. They want to
wakeboard. Right?
So, again, you don’t have to appeal specifically to your service or
product. You could easily say, “I’ll give you a killer tip for
wakeboarding.” And then in that, by saying, “Now, the only problem –
and you’ve got a big problem here – is when you wakeboard – I’m just
making this up, Lane, so excuse me if I get the terminology wrong –
when most people are wakeboarding and they’ve got that big pulley on
their boat, they’re cracking their boat. So, how can you enjoy all those
tips I just gave you, unless you have a solution to that problem? Well,
here’s the solution to that problem.”
That ties in perfectly. And as Lane if I’m right, if that works. Ask him
what he thinks.

Josh: 	This is from Richard. He says, “How can we fit video scarcity into an
evergreen system that we really want to keep releasing?” You’ve kind
of already answered this.
Jon: 	Yeah. All my products are evergreen. All I’m saying, in the scarcity, is
2 things: 1) I don’t know how long it will be online; and 2) I don’t know
how long I’ll keep it free.
Someday, I may take that video and charge people to watch it,
because it’s full of tips. People are paying a lot of money for tips that
are that good.
So, if my video’s loaded with valuable information, I may, indeed,
charge for it one day. I don’t know.
Here’s what’s really cool. Video sales letters run their course 6 to 9
months, with a lot of traffic, and a year and a half without a lot of traffic.
Let’s say a year. So, you create a video sales letter, it works great,
and it starts dying off at a year because it reached critical mass. You
could take that same exact video sales letter, put it into your product,
and give it as a $49 value bonus. So, you are, in a sense, selling it,
but you’re just giving it away for free. You’re adding it to the bottom
line. You can say, “This is also going to include this,” and add it to the
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

bottom line. That’s completely worth it, if you’ve got a lot of tips. Of
course, you’d have to take out the pitch.
So, that’s where I’m coming from that. But the on thing that you can
definitely say is, “I don’t know how long this video will be online.” Well,
you don’t. If you go down tomorrow, the internet connection could
break, eventually, sad to say, eventually you’re going to pass on and
it’s not going to be online anymore.
So, we’re using clever terms to not lie. In other words, I don’t know
how long this is going to be online. I don’t. It could be online 10-
million years, it could be online 30 days.
That’s how you build scarcity. Did that help?

-- 6 of 8 --

7
Remember, perfection is the enemy of progress. You’ve heard me say
this every single session. Perfection is the enemy of progress.
All you want to do here is progress. You’re going to start from point
zero, where you have absolutely nothing, to having a working,
functional VSL online. It doesn’t matter that it’s perfect, it doesn’t
matter that not every little thing is exactly where it needs to be. Tweak
things as they go.
Once you get it online, you’ll start seeing how effective it is for making
sales.
So, with that being the big end goal, understand that the reason why
we take this one week at a time, and give you all of the videos online,
is so that you can go back and go, “Okay, let’s take this one step at a
time. And if I have any questions, I’ll write them down and I’ll ask Jon
about it during the next section.”
That’s entirely the way to handle overwhelm.
My mom used to say, “Take the most difficult thing first, and then work
your way down. Or just take one step at a time.
So, small moves. That’s the key to overwhelm.
There’s a lot of stuff here. I want to over-deliver. And I’m trying not to
overwhelm. I realize that during these kind of calls, you’re getting hit
with a lot of stuff. But that’s exactly why it’s all online within 24 hours,
so that you have the whole week to absorb it. You can take it back.
Let me just say this to everyone on the call. If you will do the
assignments as we’ve written them out, and you will just do the work,
the “do the work” phrase, I think that comes from Alcoholics
Anonymous or some psychological thing, I don’t know, but I used to
hear it all the time from a friend of mine. “I’m doing the work. I’m
doing the work.” And that was a great mantra.
Just do the work, just go through the assignments, then you’ll end up
with something that will absolutely help you. And it doesn’t have to be
perfect. You can tweak it along the way. And once you get the hang
of it, it’s pretty simple.
So, it comes across as overwhelming, but that’s why you have the
week.
